title: Address To An Illegitimate Child.
content:
Thou's welcome, wean, mischanter fa' me,
        If ought of thee, or of thy mammy,
        Shall ever daunton me, or awe me,
            My sweet wee lady,
        Or if I blush when thou shalt ca' me
            Tit-ta or daddy.

        Wee image of my bonny Betty,
        I, fatherly, will kiss and daut thee,
        As dear and near my heart I set thee
            Wi' as gude will
        As a' the priests had seen me get thee
            That's out o' hell.

        What tho' they ca' me fornicator,
        An' tease my name in kintry clatter:
        The mair they talk I'm kent the better,
            E'en let them clash;
        An auld wife's tongue's a feckless matter
            To gie ane fash.

        Sweet fruit o' mony a merry dint,
        My funny toil is now a' tint,
        Sin' thou came to the warl asklent,
            Which fools may scoff at;
        In my last plack thy part's be in't
            The better ha'f o't.

        An' if thou be what I wad hae thee,
        An' tak the counsel I sall gie thee,
        A lovin' father I'll be to thee,
            If thou be spar'd;
        Thro' a' thy childish years I'll e'e thee,
            An' think't weel war'd.

        Gude grant that thou may ay inherit
        Thy mither's person, grace, an' merit,
        An' thy poor worthless daddy's spirit,
            Without his failins;
        'Twill please me mair to hear an' see it
            Than stocket mailens.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Burns represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Burns's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
